did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning framework for skin cancer detection from dermoscopic images. The method includes a Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) for preprocessing (hair removal and lesion enhancement), a dual-branch classifier combining a ConvNet (ResNet-18) for local features and a Vision Transformer (ViT) for global features, and late fusion via concatenation. Results were obtained on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving 98% accuracy and 97.5% F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel hybrid deep learning framework for skin cancer detection, including original contributions such as a Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) and a dual-branch CNN-ViT classifier. It details the methodology, dataset (HAM10000), experimental setup, and reports new empirical results (98% accuracy, 97.5% F1-score) and ablation studies, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Early Detection of Skin Cancer from Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning framework for the early detection of skin cancer from dermoscopic images to address challenges like hair artifacts and low contrast. The system utilizes a Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) for preprocessing, followed by a dual-branch classifier combining a ResNet-18 ConvNet for local features and a Vision Transformer (ViT) for global features. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven lesion categories. The proposed architecture achieved an accuracy of 98% and an F1-score of 97.5%, outperforming baseline CNN and pure Transformer models. Ablation studies confirmed that the integration of DCRN preprocessing and late fusion of CNN and ViT features significantly enhanced performance. The results demonstrate the framework's robustness and potential as a clinical decision support tool for reducing false negatives in melanoma detection.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnosis (benign vs. malignant) and multi-class lesion classification (7 classes: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, normal, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and multi-class classification (7 lesion categories) using a hybrid CNN-ViT framework on the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs probability distributions via Softmax for class prediction. Metrics reported include accuracy (98%), F1-score (97.5%), precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public collection of multi-source dermoscopic images used for model building and evaluation. It contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven different categories of skin lesions.

use.training: Partitioned into 80% of the dataset for training, with augmentation via random rotations and horizontal/vertical flips.
use.validation: Partitioned into 10% of the dataset for validation.
use.testing: Partitioned into 10% of the dataset for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, F1-score, and AUC metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.dataset_description: 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven lesion categories (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, normal, vasc). Split: 80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing.

Proposed Model (DCRN + CNN + ViT + Fusio.description: Hair Removal + DCRN + CNN + ViT + Fusion (concatenation)
Proposed Model (DCRN + CNN + ViT + Fusio.notes: Overall accuracy 98%, F1-score 97.5%. Outperformed baselines. Tested under illumination/contrast/noise variations maintaining >96.5% accuracy and >95.8% F1-score.

Ablation Study - Gating Fusion.description: Hair Removal + DCRN + CNN + ViT + Fusion (gating)

Robustness Test - Illumination/Contrast/.description: Proposed model tested under illumination, contrast & noise variations

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (DCRN + Dual-Branch CNN-ViT)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; Tiny-ViT selected for efficiency without requiring large-scale pretraining.
architecture_used.input_size: 3 x 224 x 224 (RGB images resized and normalized)

architecture_details.preprocessing: Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) for hair removal and lesion enhancement; morphological hair-detection and inpainting.

layers: Conv1 + BN + ReLU + MaxPool (7x7 kernel, stride 2)
cnn_branch.output: Global Average Pooling -> Linear Projection (256 dim CNN embedding)

architecture_details.fusion: Late fusion via concatenation of CNN and ViT embeddings (512 dim combined vector)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Two-layer MLP (Multilayer Perceptron) with dropout regularization (Dropout=0.5) and SoftMax activation

training_details.loss_function: Class-weighted loss function (weighting factor for malignant lesions)
training_details.augmentation: Random rotations, horizontal and vertical flips
training_details.hardware: Windows 10 laptop, 16GB RAM, NVIDIA GTX 1660 GPU

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (DCRN + ResNet-18 + Tiny-ViT)
Hair Removal + DCRN + CNN + ViT + Fusion (gating)
preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion of input dermoscopic images
Morphological hair detection using loop closure algorithm and morphological closing (dilation and erosion with disk-shaped kernel)
Hair artifact removal via thresholding to create a binary hair mask
Dilation of the hair mask to ensure continuity of detected hair structures
Image inpainting using the TELEA algorithm (isophote-driven diffusion) to restore occluded regions
Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) for lesion enhancement, boundary sharpening, and texture restoration using Multiscale Residual Aggregation Blocks (MRABs) with parallel wide convolutions
Resizing of enhanced images to 224 × 224 pixels
Normalization of input images for the classification branch

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training dataset (80% of HAM10000) to augment data diversity.
Applied to the training dataset (80% of HAM10000) to augment data diversity.
Used during training to address class imbalance by weighting malignant lesions more heavily, serving as a form of class balancing augmentation.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning framework, integrating a Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) for preprocessing and a dual-branch CNN-ViT classifier, achieved an accuracy of 98% and an F1-score of 97.5% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The framework outperformed existing baseline models, including pure CNNs (e.g., ResNet101 at 92%), pure Transformers (e.g., ViT at 93.75%), and MobileNetV2 (93.11%).

Ablation studies confirmed that the combination of DCRN preprocessing and late fusion of CNN and ViT features yielded the highest performance (98% accuracy), surpassing configurations with only hair removal, only DCRN, or gating-based fusion.

The model demonstrated robustness to real-world imaging variations, maintaining an accuracy of over 96.5% and an F1-score of over 95.8% under conditions of illumination changes, noise, Gaussian smoothing, and low-light scenarios.
Grad-CAM analysis indicated that the model focuses on clinically relevant malignant features, such as irregular lesion borders, asymmetrical pigment structures, and dark chromatic clusters.
The study acknowledges a limitation in that qualitative assessment by dermatology experts was not included, which is identified as future work to reinforce clinical reliability and interpretability.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is a multi-source dataset with known class imbalance (particularly for malignant classes like melanoma), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or single-center clinical settings.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific distribution.

The paper acknowledges a significant limitation: qualitative assessment by dermatology experts was not included, which limits the clinical reliability and interpretability validation of the Grad-CAM attention maps.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported for the performance comparisons against baseline models, making it difficult to assess if the observed improvements (e.g., 98% vs 93.75%) are statistically significant.

The hardware used for training and evaluation was a consumer-grade laptop (NVIDIA GTX 1660 GPU, 16GB RAM), which may not reflect the computational requirements for deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments or large-scale processing.

The preprocessing step uses a Deep Contrast Restoration Network (DCRN) and morphological hair removal; however, the robustness of this preprocessing pipeline to diverse hair types, skin tones, and imaging artifacts across different dermoscopic devices is not thoroughly validated beyond controlled perturbations.

The model achieves very high performance (98% accuracy, 97.5% F1-score), which may indicate potential data leakage or overly optimistic results given the lack of rigorous cross-validation or external testing.
